coredata.dc:title: Porous silica nanoparticles capped with polyethylenimine/green carbon dots for pH/redox responsive drug release
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianqian
Li, Xinli
Peng, Lichao
Zou, Xueyan
Zhao, Yanbao
coredata.dc:description: Porous silica (pSiO2) nanoparticles (NPs) were prepared by hydrolysis method and engineered with disulfide-linked gatekeepers for controlled drug release. The pSiO2 NPs with large pores have an average diameter of 60 nm and are suitable for loading drug. Cysteine (Cys) molecules as disulfide-linkers were grafting on the surface of pSiO2 carriers. After loading doxorubicin (DOX), polyethylenimine (PEI) and green carbon dots (gCDs) were used to cap the drug-loading pores. The PEI/gCDs shell can be detached from the surface of pSiO2 carriers at tumor-relevant glutathione (GSH) levels and facilitate the release of the encapsulated cargo. Meanwhile, the PEI chains would be highly protonated and lead to the swelling of PEI layer at low pH value. The pSiO2-PEI/gCDs displayed good pH/redox-responsive release behavior and gCDs can be used to monitor the drug release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous silica nanoparticles
pH/redox responsive
Drug delivery
Green carbon dots
Fluorescent monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and modification of pSiO2 carriers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of gCDs (a), pSiO2-NH2 (b), pSiO2-Cy (c) and pSiO2-PEI/gCDs sample. HRTEM and histograms of size distribution of gCDs sample (insert).
XPS spectra (a) and high-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (c) spectra of gCDs sample.
FTIR spectra (a) and zeta potential (b) of pSiO2-NH2, pSiO2-Cy and pSiO2-PEI/gCDs sample.
The DOX release kinetics from DOX-loaded pSiO2-PEI/gCDs in PBS buffer under different GSH concentration (a) and different pH values (b).
UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra and photos with/without excitation at 365 nm (insert) of gCDs solution and (a) and PL emission spectra of gCDs and pSiO2-PEI/gCDs under 410 nm.
PL intensity of pSiO2-PEI/gCDs (a) and release medium (b) with release time at 10 mg/mL GSH.
The scheme of the preparation, engineering and drug release of pSiO2 carriers.

head.ce:title: Porous silica nanoparticles capped with polyethylenimine/green carbon dots for pH/redox responsive drug release

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanomaterials, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Nanomaterials, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The general procedure for fabrication of porous silica carrier and its pH-controlled drug release.

Porous silica (pSiO2) nanoparticles (NPs) were prepared by hydrolysis method and engineered with disulfide-linked gatekeepers for controlled drug release. The pSiO2 NPs with large pores have an average diameter of 60 nm and are suitable for loading drug. Cysteine (Cys) molecules as disulfide-linkers were grafting on the surface of pSiO2 carriers. After loading doxorubicin (DOX), polyethylenimine (PEI) and green carbon dots (gCDs) were used to cap the drug-loading pores. The PEI/gCDs shell can be detached from the surface of pSiO2 carriers at tumor-relevant glutathione (GSH) levels and facilitate the release of the encapsulated cargo. Meanwhile, the PEI chains would be highly protonated and lead to the swelling of PEI layer at low pH value. The pSiO2-PEI/gCDs displayed good pH/redox-responsive release behavior and gCDs can be used to monitor the drug release.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite considerable progress in therapeutic strategies, chemotherapy still is one of the most frequently used methods for cancer therapy in clinic [1]. Unfortunately, most conventional anticancer drugs in free formulation generally encounter poor solubility, nonspecific absorption, premature degradation and negligible recognition of cancerous cells, thereby leading to severe toxic adverse effect on healthy ones and low bioavailability. Drug delivery systems encapsulating cancer drugs within suitable carriers can improve many pharmacological properties of free drugs and reduce the toxic side effects [2]. During the past few years, a large number of drug carriers, such as liposomes [3], polymers [4], hydrogels [5], inorganic nanoparticles [6] and mesoporous silica [7] were designed to realize the selective release of the drug and improve the drug efficiency.

Among current drug-delivery systems, porous silica (pSiO2) nanoparticles (NPs) have been demonstrated to be promising drug carriers on account of their tunable pore size, high surface area, favorable biocompatibility, and modifiable surface [8,9]. However, for unmodified pSiO2 carriers, the physically loaded drug would suffer unexpected premature release during the blood circulation and low level of drug accumulation in the target cancer cells. It is well known that the tumor microenvironment is usually featured by relatively low pH (~5.0), over-expressed enzyme and higher intracellular concentration of glutathione (2–8 mM) [10,11]. To achieve explosive release of the drug at the target sites and minimize the side effects, various capping agents as “gatekeepers” have been designed to block the pores of pSiO2 carriers and these capping agents were sensitive to the internal/external stimuli, such as enzymes [12], pH [13] and redox [14]. In the blood circulation, capping agents could efficiently entrap the drugs in the pore channels to prevent drug leakage while detaching by external stimuli to release the trapped drug after reaching the target site. For example, Huang et al. fabricated dual-responsive hollow silica carriers with excellent controlled release property [15].

Apart from improving the controlled release, combination of multifunctional moieties with the silica carriers has also attracted growing research interest. For instance, fluorescent functionalization is widely used to investigate the drug release process [16]. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) have good biocompatibility, tunable emission wavelengths, and excellent photostability, which are favorable in the biomedical field [17]. Here, we combined the advantages of a polymer capped pSiO2 NPs and green carbon dots (gCDs) to fabricate a dual-responsive pSiO2 based carriers for drug delivery. The pores of pSiO2 carriers were utilized to load drugs and the polyethylenimine (PEI) and gCDs were conjugated on the surface of pSiO2 carriers to block the loaded drugs via cystine (Cys) linker. The PEI molecules give pSiO2 carriers the pH-responsive property and the disulfide bonds in Cys linkers could be cleaved by GSH. In addition, the gCDs can be used to monitor the release of drug (Scheme 1 ).

Polyethylenimine (PEI, MW = 600, 99%), cystine (Cys), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl), n-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), glutathione (GSH), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were obtained from Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), citric acid (CA), urea, cyclohexane, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were received from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Ethanol, HCl (37%), NaOH and NH4OH (30%) were supplied from Luoyang Chemicals (Luoyang, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without any purification. Distilled water was used in all experiment.

Briefly, citric acid (1.0 g) and urea (1.2 g) were mixed and grinded to power in mortar. Then the mixture was dispersed by ultrasonic treatment into 10 mL of PEI solution (0.1 g/mL) and subsequently transferred to a Teflon autoclave. After heating at 200 °C for 2 h, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. The obtained gray sample was fully grinded before adding a certain volume of water. The reaction mixture was centrifuged to remove large particles and the supernatant was dialyzed using a membrane with MWCO 3 kDa followed by freeze-drying to get gCDs sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and modification of pSiO2 carriers
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of amine functionalized pSiO2 (pSiO2-NH2) was according to our previous work [18]. Typically, 0.5 g of CTAB, 70 mL of H2O, 5 mL of ethanol, 15 mL of cyclohexane, and 0.8 mL of NH3·H2O were mixed to form emulsion at room temperature. Then the mixture of TEOS (2.5 mL), APTES (0.1 mL) and ethanol (2 mL) was dropped into above emulsion and reacted for 4 h at 50 °C. The pSiO2-NH2 was obtained by centrifuging, washing, and removing CTAB with hot ethanol-HCl solution.

For grafting Cys, 9 mL of DMSO and 27 mL of DMF were mixed in three-neck flask at room temperature. Then 180 mg of EDC and 140 mg of NHS was added to the mixed solution and stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, 110 mg of Cys was also added to above solution to activate carboxyl groups. After activating for 24 h, 60 mg of pSiO2-NH2 was added and stirred for another 24 h. Finally, Cys-grafted pSiO2 (pSiO2-Cys) sample was obtained by centrifuging, washing, and drying.

Morphology of the prepared pSiO2 and gCDs samples was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA, England) analysis was further employed to analyze the elemental composition of the gCD sample. Specific surface area and pore size distribution were calculated from nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms measured on a specific surface and porosity analyzer (Quadrasorb SI, America) at 77 K. UV–vis absorption was measured on a PE-Lambda35 spectrophotometer (America) at room temperature. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Germany) with the KBr pellet technique. Zeta-potential values were examined on a Zetasizer (nano ZS, America) at 25 °C. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (JY HORIBA FluoroLog-3, France).

The loading of DOX into pSiO2-Cys is static adsorption. Typically, DOX·HCl was dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) to form stock solution with concentration of 1 mg/mL. Then, 10 mg of pSiO2-Cys was dispersed in 5 mL of DOX solution to load DOX. After agitated at 37 °C overnight in the dark, the pSiO2-Cys loaded with DOX (denoted as DOX-loaded pSiO2-Cys) was centrifuged and washed extensively to remove the excess unloaded DOX. The supernatant was collected and measured by UV absorption at 480 nm to calculate amount of loaded DOX.

For PEI/gCDs capping: 160 mg of DOX-loaded pSiO2-Cys was dispersed into 90 mL of DMF to form suspension. Then 156 mg of DEC and 100 mg of NHS were added to above suspension to activate the carboxyl groups of the grafted Cys linkers for 30 min. Afterwards, 3 mL of PEI solution (0.2 g/mL) and 10 mg of gCDs were added to above suspension and the resulting suspension was shaken for another 6 h at 25 °C to obtain DOX-loaded pSiO2-PEI/gCDs sample followed by centrifugation, washing and drying.

The in vitro release of DOX from DOX-loaded pSiO2-PEI/gCDs was studied at 37 °C under different conditions: (i) PBS at pH 5.6 and pH 7.4; (ii) PBS with different GSH concentrations (0, 0.1, 1, and 10 mM); (iii) PBS containing 10 mM GSH at pH 5.6. Typically, 5 mg of DOX-loaded pSiO2-PEI/gCDs was dispersed in 5 mL of PBS solution and sealed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 8000 Da). The sealed dialysis bag was dialyzed in corresponding PBS medium (50 mL) at 37 °C in the dark. At the given time intervals, 5.0 mL of the release medium was taken out, while equal volume of fresh medium was added back to the original release medium. The amount of released DOX was evaluated based UV absorbance and each release experiment was repeated three times.

TEM images were used to characterize the morphologies of gCDs and pSiO2 samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a, gCDs sample is well dispersed and the histograms of size distribution (insert) indicates that the average diameter is about 1.4 nm. The HRTEM image (inset) presents a crystal spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [19]. The pSiO2-NH2 sample presents spherical shape with dendritic large pores and has an average size of 60 nm (Fig. 1b). After Cys grafting, the porous structure is still clearly visible (Fig. 1c). After loading DOX and coating PEI/gCDs, there is no obvious change in morphology and porous structure (Fig. 1d).

XPS measurements with high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra were conducted to the element composition of gCD sample. It can be seen that the prepared gCDs sample is mainly composed of C, N and O with the binding energy of 284.7 eV, 398.7 eV and 530.4 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 a). A high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be divided into three peaks (Fig. 2b), which indicate the presence of C-C/C = C (283.7 eV), C-N (285.0 eV) and C = O (287.4 eV) bonds on the surface of gCDs [20]. N1s spectrum display two peaks at 399.3 eV and 398.7 eV, corresponding to N-H and C-N-C, respectively. The O1s spectrum reveals the presence of C-O (531.2 eV) and C = O (530.2 eV) [21].

Fig. 3 a gives the FT-IR spectra of the prepared pSiO2-NH2, pSiO2-Cy, pSiO2-PEI/gCDs and gCDs samples. For pSiO2-NH2, the characteristic bands at 1080 and 794 cm−1 are associated with the amorphous silica Si–O–Si vibration, and the peak at 955 cm−1 is attributed to the Si–OH bending vibration. The weak peak at 1462 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of N−H, confirming the presence of amine groups [22]. After grafting Cys, there appear two peaks at 1525 and 1410 cm−1 of amide II absorption, indicating the forming amide bonds between carboxyl groups of Cys and amino groups of silica [23]. For pSiO2-PEI/gCDs, the peaks at 2933, 1668 and1450 cm-1are coincided with –CH2 of PEI, and C–H and C = O of gCDs, respectively [24], illustrating the successful modification of PEI and gCDs. Zeta potentials are also used to characterize the surface features of the prepared samples, shown in Fig. 3b. The zeta potential of bare pSiO2 sample is −20.5 mV due to the presence of Si–OH [18]. After introducing amino groups, the zeta potential is increased to +34.1 mV. When Cys is grafting, the zeta potential is decreased to +28.2 mV, due to introducing negatively charged carboxyl groups. After PEI and gCDs capping, the zeta potential is increased to +42.0 mV due to the presence of positively charged amino groups. The change in particle zeta potentials confirms the successful modification of each step.

The pSiO2 carrier with large pores is beneficial for the drug loading, and its loading capacity of Dox is about 19.6%, and that of gCDs is about 2.3%. The disulfide bonds of Cys linker would be cleaved by the high GSH level in the tumor microenvironment and lead to the detachment of PEI/gCDs layer. Therefore, the release of DOX from DOX-loaded pSiO2-PEI/gCDs carrier could be triggered by GSH stimuli. Fig. 4 a gives the influence of GSH on the cumulative releasing profile of DOX at pH 7.4. It is observed that the pSiO2-PEI/gCDs carrier displays an excellent GSH-responsive release. In the absence of GSH, the released amount is only 16.3% within 10 h. Upon addition of 0.1 mM GSH, the release is increased to 30%. The DOX release is gradually enhanced as the concentration of GSH increased. At 10 mM, the release reaches an equilibrium within 4 h, and the released amount is 63.2% and there is no burst phenomenon, which indicates that the PEI/gCDs layer has a good capping effect.

It is well known that the low pH value is one of features of tumor cells. Under weakly acidic condition, the PEI chains would be highly protonated and lead to the swelling of the PEI layer. Fig. 4b depicts the cumulative release of DOX under different pH values. At pH 5.6, a rapid release of DOX is observed and the release amount of DOX is significantly increased to 36.7%. In this case, the PEI layer is still conjugated on the surface of pSiO2 carriers, the enhanced release is relatively limited compared with presence of GSH. The rapid release is obtained under GSH/pH dual-stimuli and the release amount is up to 82.5% at pH5.6 and 10 mM GSH, which may be due to the detachment of PEI and gCDs. In addition, the DOX release follows the first order kinetics model with high coefficients (R2), which are higher than 0.98.

Here, gCDs are also employed as fluorescent probe to monitor the DOX release. Fig. 5 a gives UV absorption, excitation and PL emission spectra of gCDs sample. It is observed that the gCDs solution is transparent and yellow under natural light and there appear two absorption peaks located at 330 and 397 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of gCDs [25]. At the excitation of 365 nm, the gCDs solution presents green fluorescence and the excitation peak of gCDs solution appears at 410 nm. At the excitation of 410 nm, both gCDs and pSiO2-PEI/gCDs display the emission peak at 540 nm with different PL intensity, indicating the successful of capping gCDs. The PL intensity of pSiO2-PEI/gCDs is weaker than that of gCDs, which may be related to the interaction between gCDs, Cys and PEI.

The modification of gCDs endows the pSiO2 carrier with fluorescent property, which would be used to monitor the drug release process. Fig. 6 gives the PL spectra of pSiO2-PEI/gCDs and release medium with release time. It is visible that the PL intensity of the pSiO2-PEI/gCDs is reduced, while that of the release medium is increased with the release time going in the presence of 10 mM GSH. This is because that the breakup of disulfide bonds of Cys linkers by GSH would induce the gCDs falling off from the pSiO2 carrier and resulting in the decrease in the PL intensity of the pSiO2-PEI/gCDs carrier. As expected, the detached gCDs would go into the release medium and cause the enhanced PL intensity of the release medium. Therefore, the change in PL intensity would be used to track the carrier and monitor drug release.

In the study, pSiO2 carriers with large-pores were successfully developed via a one-pot hydrolysis method and Cys as disulfide-linker was conjugated on the surface of pSiO2 carrier. After loaded DOX, PEI and gCDs were used to entrap drugs in pores of pSiO2 carrier and pSiO2-PEI/gCDs shows pH/redox-responsive drug release. Moreover, the gCDs can be used to monitor the process of drug release.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Medical Interdisciplinary training Program of Henan University in 2019 (CJ1205A0240013) and Interdisciplinary Research for First-Class Discipline Construction Project of Henan University (grant No. 2019YLXKJC04).
